steps.title: Find the pattern that contradicts the hypothesis
steps.content:
The hypothesis predicts that as government efficiency increases, GDP growth becomes relatively more important to urban expansion (higher %), while population growth becomes less important (lower %).

The graph shows Region 1's GDP contribution fell sharply from 38% to 28%, while its population contribution rose from 65% to 75% — the exact opposite of what efficiency improvement would produce.

steps.title: Apply the hypothesis to Region 1's trend
steps.content:
If the hypothesis holds, then a decrease in GDP's importance relative to population signifies a decline in government efficiency, not an increase.

Region 1's reversal (GDP down, population up) is direct evidence that its government efficiency weakened, not strengthened, over the 2000–2014 period.

The other choices either claim Region 1 became more efficient despite this contradictory data, claim both regions improved when Region 1 deteriorated, or hedge with “most but not all” when discussing aggregate regional data that shows one clear trend.

That confirms B is correct.
